title: Chest Pain Emergency - Acute Myocardial Infarction
description: A 58-year-old male presents to the emergency department with severe chest pain. This scenario tests rapid assessment, ECG interpretation, and emergency cardiac care protocols.
patient_complaint: I have severe crushing pain in my chest that radiates to my left arm and jaw. It started about 30 minutes ago and feels like someone is sitting on my chest.
presenting_symptoms: Severe chest pain (8/10), shortness of breath, diaphoresis, nausea, pain radiating to left arm and jaw
medical_history: Hypertension (10 years), Type 2 Diabetes (8 years), Hyperlipidemia, Former smoker (quit 5 years ago), Family history of heart disease
medications: Lisinopril 10mg daily, Metformin 500mg twice daily, Atorvastatin 20mg daily, Aspirin 81mg daily
social_history: Works as accountant, sedentary lifestyle, occasional alcohol (2-3 drinks/week), no illicit drugs
family_history: Father had MI at age 55, Mother has hypertension, Brother has type 2 diabetes
vital_signs:
{
  "blood_pressure": "165/95 mmHg",
  "heart_rate": "110 bpm",
  "respiratory_rate": "24 breaths/min",
  "temperature": "98.6°F",
  "oxygen_saturation": "94% on room air",
  "pain_score": "8/10"
}
physical_exam_findings: Diaphoretic, anxious appearing, regular tachycardic rhythm, clear lungs bilaterally, no murmurs rubs or gallops, abdomen soft non-tender, no peripheral edema
lab_results:
{
  "troponin_i": "2.5 ng/mL (elevated)",
  "ck_mb": "15 ng/mL (elevated)",
  "bnp": "250 pg/mL (elevated)",
  "glucose": "180 mg/dL",
  "lipid_panel": "LDL 145, HDL 35, Triglycerides 200",
  "cmp": "Within normal limits"
}
imaging_results: ECG shows ST elevation in leads II, III, aVF (inferior wall MI), reciprocal changes in leads I, aVL, V1-V3
differential_diagnosis:
1. Acute Coronary Syndrome (STEMI)
2. Unstable Angina
3. Aortic Dissection
4. Pulmonary Embolism
5. Pericarditis
treatment_plan:
1. Immediate aspirin 324mg chewable
2. Nitroglycerin sublingual
3. Morphine for pain
4. Oxygen if SpO2 <90%
5. Activate cardiac catheterization lab
6. Prepare for PCI
7. Start beta-blocker and ACE inhibitor
8. Continue statin therapy
expected_actions:
1. Perform rapid assessment within 2 minutes
2. Obtain 12-lead ECG within 10 minutes
3. Administer aspirin immediately
4. Activate STEMI protocol
5. Prepare for reperfusion therapy
6. Monitor vital signs continuously
7. Provide pain management
8. Document timeline of care
red_flags: Severe chest pain with diaphoresis, ST elevation on ECG, elevated troponin, hemodynamic instability
educational_objectives:
1. Recognize acute MI presentation
2. Interpret ECG findings
3. Implement STEMI protocol
4. Manage acute cardiac pain
5. Coordinate cardiac catheterization
6. Provide appropriate medications
clinical_guidelines: ACC/AHA Guidelines for STEMI, AHA Emergency Cardiac Care

title: Pediatric Seizure - Status Epilepticus
description: A 4-year-old child presents with ongoing seizure activity. This scenario tests pediatric emergency management, seizure protocols, and family communication skills.
patient_complaint: Child has been shaking uncontrollably for the past 15 minutes and won't wake up!
presenting_symptoms: Generalized tonic-clonic seizure activity lasting 15+ minutes, unresponsive, cyanotic lips, drooling
medical_history: Known epilepsy diagnosed at age 2, previous seizures well-controlled on medication, developmental delay, no other chronic conditions
medications: Levetiracetam 200mg twice daily, emergency diazepam rectal gel PRN
social_history: Lives with both parents, attends preschool, no known allergies, up to date on immunizations
family_history: Mother has migraines, no family history of epilepsy or other neurological conditions
vital_signs:
{
  "blood_pressure": "110/70 mmHg",
  "heart_rate": "145 bpm",
  "respiratory_rate": "30 breaths/min",
  "temperature": "101.2°F",
  "oxygen_saturation": "88% on room air",
  "blood_glucose": "85 mg/dL"
}
physical_exam_findings: Active tonic-clonic seizure activity, eyes deviated upward, increased muscle tone, cyanotic extremities, post-ictal confusion, no obvious trauma
imaging_results: CT head shows no acute abnormalities, previous MRI shows temporal lobe changes consistent with epilepsy
differential_diagnosis:
1. Status Epilepticus (most likely)
2. Febrile Seizure
3. Intracranial hemorrhage
4. Metabolic encephalopathy
5. Toxic ingestion
6. Meningitis/Encephalitis
treatment_plan:
1. Secure airway, administer oxygen
2. Establish IV access
3. Administer lorazepam IV
4. If persists, load with fosphenytoin
5. Consider second-line agents (levetiracetam, valproic acid)
6. Monitor continuous EEG
7. Investigate precipitating factors
8. Admit to PICU for monitoring
expected_actions:
1. Assess ABCs within 1 minute
2. Administer oxygen and monitor vitals
3. Establish IV access
4. Administer benzodiazepine
5. Prepare second-line medications
6. Communicate with family
7. Document seizure timeline
8. Arrange ICU admission
red_flags: Seizure lasting >5 minutes, cyanosis, fever, altered mental status, failure to respond to initial treatment
educational_objectives:
1. Manage pediatric status epilepticus
2. Administer appropriate seizure medications
3. Communicate with distressed family
4. Monitor for complications
5. Identify seizure triggers
6. Coordinate pediatric ICU care
clinical_guidelines: American Epilepsy Society Guidelines, Pediatric Advanced Life Support

title: Sepsis Recognition and Management
description: A 72-year-old female presents with altered mental status and signs of infection. This scenario tests sepsis recognition, early goal-directed therapy, and critical care management.
patient_complaint: My mother is confused and has been very weak for the past day. She has a fever and hasn't been eating or drinking.
presenting_symptoms: Altered mental status, fever, weakness, decreased urine output, rapid breathing, confusion
medical_history: Type 2 Diabetes (15 years), Hypertension (10 years), Chronic kidney disease stage 3, Osteoarthritis, Recent urinary catheter placement (3 days ago)
medications: Insulin glargine, Metoprolol 50mg twice daily, Acetaminophen PRN, No recent antibiotics
social_history: Lives alone, home health aide visits twice daily, recently discharged from rehab after hip fracture, no smoking or alcohol
family_history: Father had diabetes, Mother had hypertension
vital_signs:
{
  "blood_pressure": "85/50 mmHg",
  "heart_rate": "125 bpm",
  "respiratory_rate": "28 breaths/min",
  "temperature": "102.8°F",
  "oxygen_saturation": "91% on room air",
  "urine_output": "15 mL in past 4 hours"
}
physical_exam_findings: Confused, disoriented to time and place, dry mucous membranes, tachycardic with regular rhythm, lungs clear bilateral, abdomen soft with suprapubic tenderness, urinary catheter in place with cloudy urine, skin turgor decreased
lab_results:
{
  "wbc": "18.5 K/uL (elevated)",
  "hemoglobin": "10.2 g/dL",
  "platelets": "180 K/uL",
  "creatinine": "2.1 mg/dL (elevated from baseline 1.5)",
  "bun": "45 mg/dL",
  "lactate": "4.2 mmol/L (elevated)",
  "glucose": "220 mg/dL",
  "urinalysis": "Positive for nitrites, leukocyte esterase, >50 WBC/HPF"
}
imaging_results: Chest X-ray clear, Abdominal X-ray shows no obstruction, Renal ultrasound shows no hydronephrosis
correct_diagnosis: Septic Shock secondary to Catheter-Associated Urinary Tract Infection
differential_diagnosis:
1. Septic Shock (UTI source)
2. Diabetic Ketoacidosis
3. Acute Kidney Injury
4. Pulmonary Embolism
5. Myocardial Infarction
6. Dehydration
treatment_plan:
1. Immediate fluid resuscitation (30 mL/kg crystalloid)
2. Broad-spectrum antibiotics within 1 hour
3. Remove urinary catheter and obtain culture
4. Vasopressor support if needed
5. Monitor lactate clearance
6. Repeat labs and vitals
7. ICU admission
8. Source control measures
expected_actions:
1. Recognize sepsis within 5 minutes
2. Initiate sepsis protocol
3. Administer fluids rapidly
4. Obtain cultures before antibiotics
5. Start broad-spectrum antibiotics
6. Monitor response to treatment
7. Arrange ICU admission
8. Document sepsis bundle completion
red_flags: Hypotension, tachycardia, altered mental status, elevated lactate, elevated WBC, recent urinary catheter
educational_objectives:
1. Recognize sepsis early
2. Implement sepsis bundles
3. Manage septic shock
4. Choose appropriate antibiotics
5. Monitor treatment response
6. Coordinate critical care
clinical_guidelines: Surviving Sepsis Campaign Guidelines, CDC Catheter-Associated UTI Prevention
